summary.intent: Resolve all Codex review threads on PR #704 in fogodev/ars-ui and get CI green after adding range validation logic to the RangeCalendar component.
summary.outcome: All review threads were resolved, the spec was updated to match the Rust implementation's range constraint validation path, and the fix was committed and pushed with CI passing on most checks.

repo: The project uses `cargo xtask spec validate` to lint/validate component spec markdown files against the implementation.
Codex review is triggered by posting '@codex review' as an issue comment via the GitHub API, and Codex signals it has seen the request by reacting with 'eyes' before opening new threads.
Review threads are resolved via the GitHub GraphQL API using `resolveReviewThread` mutation paired with `addPullRequestReviewThreadReply`.

The RangeCalendar range validation order is: same-day/span checks first, then endpoint disabled checks, then full-range unavailable scanning when any unavailable source (static `unavailable_dates` or dynamic predicate) is present.

code.finding: Contains range constraint fields: allow_single_date_range, min_range_days, max_range_days, and range_is_allowed/range_is_selectable guards.
Implements the second-click commit path with full unavailable-range scanning; the spec snippet was stale and omitted this guard path.
Spec machine snippets for RangeCalendar lacked the range constraint fields in the public Context example and the transition snippet was missing the span/unavailable validation call on second click.

workflow: Polling CI and Codex review state requires repeated `gh pr checks` and GraphQL queries with sleep intervals; 120-180s sleep cycles were used between polls.
Committing spec fixes as a Codex review follow-up with co-author attribution (`Co-authored-by: Codex`) is the project convention for AI-driven review resolutions.
Using `git diff --check` before committing catches whitespace issues introduced by patch application.

friction: Multiple long sleep-poll cycles (120-180s each) were needed to wait for CI and Codex review reactions, making the feedback loop slow.
Codex opened a new spec thread only after all previous threads were resolved and a fresh review was triggered, requiring another full patch/commit/push/resolve cycle.
The spec and implementation had drifted silently — the stale spec snippet was not caught by existing validation until Codex flagged it.
open_items: Longer workspace CI gates were still running at the end of the transcript; final green status was not confirmed.
No second Codex review was triggered after the spec fix commit was pushed — a fresh '@codex review' ping and thread resolution cycle may still be needed.
The spec drift between machine snippets and the Rust implementation suggests a need for automated spec-to-implementation conformance checking beyond `cargo xtask spec validate`.
